MongoDB is already running with TLS. Its current server certificates are nearing
expiry. Rotate the server certificates without downtime, keep the existing CA
trust unchanged, and ensure the new server certificates are valid for about one year.

Notes:
- The CA cert and key are stored in the secret mongodb-tls-ca.
- The current server cert is stored in mongodb-tls-cert.
- The openssl-toolbox pod is available to generate new certificates.

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture:
- CA cert: stored in `mongodb-tls-ca` secret with keys `ca.crt` and `ca.key`
- Server cert: stored in `mongodb-tls-cert` secret with key `server.pem` (contains both cert and private key concatenated)
- Current server cert expires in 2 days (Jun 24-26 2026), needs rotation
- Server cert has SANs for all the mongo-rs pod DNS names
- openssl-toolbox pod is available in the mongodb namespace

Plan:
1. Extract CA cert and key from the `mongodb-tls-ca` secret into the openssl-toolbox pod
2. Generate a new server key and CSR in the toolbox pod
3. Sign the CSR with the CA to get a new cert valid for 365 days
4. Update the `mongodb-tls-cert` secret with the new cert+key as `server.pem`
5. Perform a rolling restart of the StatefulSet to pick up the new cert

For zero downtime, I'll leverage MongoDB's replica set capabilities—restarting one member at a time keeps the cluster available. I need to generate the new cert, update the secret, then trigger a rolling restart of the StatefulSet so each pod picks up the new TLS cert.

Now I'll execute this in the openssl-toolbox pod, starting by extracting the CA cert and key from the secret.

content.text: The current server cert expires in 2 days. I'll rotate it using the openssl-toolbox pod to generate a new cert signed by the same CA, then do a rolling restart for zero downtime.

input.command:
# Extract CA cert and key from the secret and write to the toolbox pod
CA_CRT=$(kubectl get secret mongodb-tls-ca -n mongodb -o jsonpath='{.data.ca\.crt}' | base64 -d)
CA_KEY=$(kubectl get secret mongodb-tls-ca -n mongodb -o jsonpath='{.data.ca\.key}' | base64 -d)

# Write them into the toolbox pod
kubectl exec -n mongodb openssl-toolbox -- sh -c "cat > /tmp/ca.crt << 'CAEOF'
${CA_CRT}
CAEOF"
kubectl exec -n mongodb openssl-toolbox -- sh -c "cat > /tmp/ca.key << 'CAEOF'
${CA_KEY}
CAEOF"

echo "CA files written to toolbox"
input.description: Write CA cert and key to the openssl-toolbox pod

input.command:
# Verify CA cert in the toolbox pod
kubectl exec -n mongodb openssl-toolbox -- openssl x509 -in /tmp/ca.crt -noout -subject -dates
input.description: Verify CA cert loaded correctly in toolbox

input.command:
# Generate new server key and CSR with the same SANs as the existing cert
kubectl exec -n mongodb openssl-toolbox -- sh -c '
# Generate new RSA 2048 private key
openssl genrsa -out /tmp/server.key 2048

# Create OpenSSL config with the SANs from the existing cert
cat > /tmp/server.cnf << EOF
[req]
req_extensions = v3_req
distinguished_name = req_distinguished_name
prompt = no

[req_distinguished_name]
CN = mongo-rs

[v3_req]
keyUsage = digitalSignature, keyEncipherment
extendedKeyUsage = serverAuth, clientAuth
subjectAltName = @alt_names

[alt_names]
DNS.1 = localhost
DNS.2 = mongo
DNS.3 = mongo.mongodb
DNS.4 = mongo.mongodb.svc
DNS.5 = mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.6 = mongo-rs-0.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.7 = mongo-rs-1.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.8 = mongo-rs-2.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
EOF

# Generate CSR
openssl req -new -key /tmp/server.key -out /tmp/server.csr -config /tmp/server.cnf

echo "Key and CSR generated"
openssl req -in /tmp/server.csr -noout -subject -verify
'
input.description: Generate new server key and CSR in the toolbox pod

content.content:
Key and CSR generated
Certificate request self-signature verify OK
subject=CN=mongo-rs

tool_use_result.stdout:
Key and CSR generated
Certificate request self-signature verify OK
subject=CN=mongo-rs

input.command:
# Sign the CSR with the CA to get a new server cert valid for 365 days
kubectl exec -n mongodb openssl-toolbox -- sh -c '
# Create extensions file for the signed cert
cat > /tmp/ext.cnf << EOF
[v3_req]
keyUsage = digitalSignature, keyEncipherment
extendedKeyUsage = serverAuth, clientAuth
subjectAltName = @alt_names

[alt_names]
DNS.1 = localhost
DNS.2 = mongo
DNS.3 = mongo.mongodb
DNS.4 = mongo.mongodb.svc
DNS.5 = mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.6 = mongo-rs-0.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.7 = mongo-rs-1.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.8 = mongo-rs-2.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
EOF

openssl x509 -req \
  -in /tmp/server.csr \
  -CA /tmp/ca.crt \
  -CAkey /tmp/ca.key \
  -CAcreateserial \
  -out /tmp/server.crt \
  -days 365 \
  -extensions v3_req \
  -extfile /tmp/ext.cnf

echo ""
echo "New cert signed:"
openssl x509 -in /tmp/server.crt -noout -subject -issuer -dates -ext subjectAltName
'
input.description: Sign the CSR with the CA for 365-day validity

content.thinking:
The new certificate is signed and valid for 365 days (until Jun 24 2027). Now I need to:
1. Concatenate the cert and key into server.pem (the format expected by the existing secret)
2. Read the new server.pem from the toolbox pod
3. Update the `mongodb-tls-cert` secret with the new value
4. Do a rolling restart of the StatefulSet

Let me do the rolling restart. For a MongoDB replica set statefulset, `kubectl rollout restart` will do a rolling restart one pod at a time.

content.text: New cert is valid Jun 24 2026 → Jun 24 2027. Now concatenate cert+key into server.pem and update the secret.

input.command:
# Concatenate cert and key into server.pem (same format as existing secret)
kubectl exec -n mongodb openssl-toolbox -- sh -c '
cat /tmp/server.crt /tmp/server.key > /tmp/server.pem
echo "server.pem created, length: $(wc -c < /tmp/server.pem) bytes"
'
input.description: Create server.pem from cert and key

input.command:
# Read the new server.pem from the toolbox pod and update the Kubernetes secret
NEW_PEM=$(kubectl exec -n mongodb openssl-toolbox -- cat /tmp/server.pem)
NEW_PEM_B64=$(echo "$NEW_PEM" | base64 -w 0)

# Patch the secret with the new server.pem
kubectl patch secret mongodb-tls-cert -n mongodb \
  --type='json' \
  -p="[{\"op\": \"replace\", \"path\": \"/data/server.pem\", \"value\": \"${NEW_PEM_B64}\"}]"

echo "Secret updated"
input.description: Update the mongodb-tls-cert secret with new cert

input.command:
# Verify the secret now contains the new cert
kubectl get secret mongodb-tls-cert -n mongodb -o jsonpath='{.data.server\.pem}' | base64 -d | openssl x509 -noout -subject -dates
input.description: Verify secret has the new cert with correct expiry

content.content:
subject=CN = mongo-rs
notBefore=Jun 24 05:18:03 2026 GMT
notAfter=Jun 24 05:18:03 2027 GMT

tool_use_result.stdout:
subject=CN = mongo-rs
notBefore=Jun 24 05:18:03 2026 GMT
notAfter=Jun 24 05:18:03 2027 GMT

input.command:
# Rolling restart the StatefulSet to load the new cert (one pod at a time = no downtime)
kubectl rollout restart statefulset/mongo-rs -n mongodb
echo "Rolling restart initiated"
input.description: Trigger rolling restart of MongoDB StatefulSet

input.command:
# Wait for the rolling restart to complete
kubectl rollout status statefulset/mongo-rs -n mongodb --timeout=300s
input.description: Wait for rolling restart to complete

input.command:
# Verify all pods are running and the new cert is in use
kubectl get pods -n mongodb
echo ""
# Check cert served by mongo-rs-0 via TLS connection
kubectl exec -n mongodb openssl-toolbox -- sh -c '
openssl s_client -connect mongo-rs-0.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local:27017 \
  -CAfile /tmp/ca.crt \
  -servername mongo-rs-0.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local \
  </dev/null 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -subject -dates 2>/dev/null
'
input.description: Verify pods are running and new cert is served
